                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Understanding SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA): Execution Boundaries and Remote Data Processing
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA) enables SAP HANA to access data stored in external systems without replicating the data into SAP HANA.

External systems are connected as remote sources, and their objects can be represented inside SAP HANA as virtual tables.
From an application perspective, these objects can be queried using standard SQL statements, while the data remains in the source system and is accessed during statement execution.

Official SAP HANA Smart Data Access documentation:
Data Access in the SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database

This article explains general processing behavior and supports correct interpretation during troubleshooting.
It does not provide configuration or performance tuning guidance.


Remote Sources and Virtual Tables
With SDA, SAP HANA integrates remote data access into SQL processing.
When a query references a virtual table, SAP HANA processes the statement, and data access is performed via the connected remote source during execution.
This behavior is part of the documented SAP HANA Smart Data Access architecture.


Query Processing Context
In SAP HANA Smart Data Access, SQL statements referencing virtual tables may involve both local SAP HANA processing and operations executed against the connected remote source.
As part of query processing, operations may be executed locally in SAP HANA or against the connected remote source.
Query execution may therefore include processing steps across system boundaries as part of normal system behavior.


Support-Relevant Distinction
From a support perspective, the following distinction is essential:
A statement can be initiated and processed by SAP HANA, while parts of the effective data processing may take place outside SAP HANA via the remote source and the communication layer.
Because of this execution boundary, the status of SAP HANA services alone does not describe the complete processing path when Smart Data Access is used.


Observations in Troubleshooting
During troubleshooting, it is possible that local SAP HANA processing appears normal while statements accessing virtual tables show different response times.

This can occur because the execution path may include:

  • the SAP HANA database
  • the network communication layer
  • the connected remote source

The overall response time therefore reflects the complete end-to-end processing path.


Documented SDA Scenarios
SAP documentation and SAP Notes describe different SDA-related scenarios that can influence behavior during execution.

SDA FAQ:
https://me.sap.com/notes/2180119

Examples include:

  • Remote source object discovery error scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/2572731
  • SDA remote source connection test / communication error scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/3470273
  • Remote source object access / closed connection scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/2557181

These scenarios may occur while the SAP HANA database itself remains available.


Interpreting Monitoring
When remote sources are involved, troubleshooting should consider the full end-to-end data access path rather than only local database activity.


Support Scope Clarification
When analyzing statements accessing virtual tables, the involved components may include:

  • SAP HANA database
  • network communication
  • remote data source

Observed behavior alone is not sufficient to determine a root cause and requires end-to-end analysis across all involved components.


Conclusion
SAP HANA Smart Data Access allows access to external data without replication.
Virtual tables represent remote objects, and data access from the connected system is part of normal execution behavior.
Statements executed in SAP HANA may therefore include processing steps outside the SAP HANA database, while SAP HANA operates according to its documented architecture.
Understanding this behavior supports correct interpretation during troubleshooting.


Disclaimer
This article summarizes documented system behavior based on publicly available SAP documentation and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

Only official SAP documentation and SAP Notes are authoritative.

entry.title: Configure cluster environment using SUSE Pacemaker for HANA replication
entry.content:
In manual HANA replication configuration.We have to perform Take over/switch over manually.To automate take over/switch over.we have to configure cluster environment using SUSE Pacemaker.

 

Below are the steps
1.NFSSRV2-NFS server
2.HANADBPRI-Primary HANA DB
3.HANADBHA-Secondary/HA HANA DB
4.HANADBVHOST-Virtual IP/Floating IP(It will be moving here based on the active nodes.
Note:Virtual IP once added will not be reachable until Pacemaker is confifgured

Pre-Requisites
1.Install HANA DB
2.Configure replication
3.Below OS packages needs to be installed
zypper install -t pattern ha_sles-Mandatory
zypper in corosync-qdevice- Not Mandatory.Required only id Q devices are installed
zypper in SAPHanaSR-Mandatory
zypper in corosync-qnetd-Not Mandatory.Required only id Q devices are installed
4.Install Primary and Secondary/HA DB with Local host name.

Note:In cluster environment we install HANA DB with local hostname and not with Virtual host name.Whereas Cluster environment with other products like ASCS,ERS we will be install SAP using Virtual Host Name

 

We are using setup as below

 

Scenario 1:

Suse pacemaker will be sending heartbeat from Primary to Secondary/HA DB(Basically it is called as Ping).If we are not getting the reply for the ping.Then Pacemaker will bring up Secondary/HA DB

Scenario 2:

Split Brain scenario-Both Primary and Secondary/HA DB’s will be alive but due to some network issue replication will be broken and no heart beat signal will be passed to each other.Primary DB and Secondary/HA DB thinks that each other DB is down & dead(But both DB will be up) and try to pull up the cluster and bring the resources(Virtual IP/Floating IP) in their respective DB thinking other DB is down.This causes confusion to Suse pacemaker and network.This scenario is called as Split Brain Scenario.


To Avoid the Split Brain Scenario we have 2 solutions


1.SDB-1 shared disk is created in the virtualized platforms and it is mounted in both the platforms and status of the cluster is stored in the SDB and it very small in size almost 1mb.If SDB is disconnected then it the DB will think that network is down.If SDB is available and Hana DB is not available.Then it will think that Hana DB is down and take over/switch over will happen.


2.Q device using NFS Filesystem
Note:When the DB is down the only virtual/Floating IP will be shifted from primary to secondary/HA DB because replication is active and Data will be present in secondary.Where in SAP ASCS/ERS will be installed and complete Filesystem will be moved from Primary to HA server.
Configure replication between Primary & Secondary/HA DB.Take backup for primary and Secondary/HA DB and enable replication.

 

 

Note:
1.Scale Up-Only 1 systemwe can increase the Ram and other resources.
2.Scale Out-We can add the multiple nodes(Mater node->Worker Node->Standy node)

 

Configuration of Cluster

1.This command will work only when the above mentioned packages are installed

 

2.Ring is interface for network communication. It will be created in both HA and DR.It will automatically take up the local IP

 

3.We are following the Qdevice using NFS Server method

 

 

We are going with the Q device method
Note:The below highlighted packages mandatorily needs to be installed in the NFS Server to Run Q devices.

 

 

Current status if cluster-crm status

 

To check the live changes in cluster-crm_mon

 

Check whether watchdog is active or not.It is used to check the services running on OS level

 

Now cluster is initiated in Primary Node.Cluster is the one time activity which needs to be configured only once on 1 node and in another node we can join the existing cluster
Note:Cluster join means any cluster configuration changes made in one server will reflect into another server

 

Check whether watchdog is running or not

 

Watchdog is not running,or else it will show the line as dog
Now start the watchdog
If watchdog is not active create it
echo softdog > /etc/modules-load.d/watchdog.conf
start watchdog
systemctl restart systemd-modules-load.service

 

Put cluster into maintainence mode

 

We need to add hana related resources into the server.It can be added from any node.We need to add the resource agent(RA).RA is the utility used to perform or monitor the operations.


There are 2 resource agent

1.SAP HANA topology(Used to check whether hana services is crashed or not)

2.SAP HANA(Used to start and stop the database) are the two RA.
Note:We can add primitives in either primary or secondary server node.It will automatically sync to another server.But in this scenario I am adding primitive in 1st server.

 

Add the resource agent

 

Add the 1st primitive
primitive rsc_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 ocf:suse:SAPHanaTopology \ #Monitor the HANA services#
op monitor interval="10" timeout="600" \ #Will monitor the service for every 10 secs and should get an reply within 600 sec#
op start interval="0" timeout="600" \#Hana DB stop and start time#
op stop interval="0" timeout="300" \\#Hana DB stop and start time#
params SID="HDB" InstanceNumber="00"#HANA DB SID and instance number#
clone cln_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 rsc_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 \ #Clone of the HANA topology#

 

Once saved clone is set into secondary

 

SAP HANA Topology resource agent comes with the package zypper SAP HANA SR

 

Add 2nd primitive HANA DB Resource Agent for HANA db start and stop in primary server

 

primitive rsc_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 ocf:suse:SAPHana \
op start interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op stop interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op promote interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op monitor interval="60" role="Master" timeout="700" \
op monitor interval="61" role="Slave" timeout="700" \
params SID="HDB" InstanceNumber="00" PREFER_SITE_TAKEOVER="true" \
DUPLICATE_PRIMARY_TIMEOUT="7200" AUTOMATED_REGISTER="true" \
meta priority="100"
ms msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 rsc_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 \
meta clone-max="2" clone-node-max="1" interleave="true"

 

 

save

 

Add constraints into the primary server

Two constraints are organizing the correct placement of the virtual IP address for the client database access and the start order between the two resource agents SAPHana and SAPHanaTopology.
colocation col_admin-ip_HDB_HDB00 2000: admin-ip:Started \
msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00:Master
order ord_SAPHana_HA1_HDB10 Optional: cln_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 \
msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00

 

This will not do any changes after saving as it is only rules.
Note:The extra lines will appear after adding primitive 1 and primitive 2 and saving it in the file

 

 

Note:

 

Cluster delete command

Note if you are stuck inbetween and want to start from first you need to remove the cluster by below

Next part will be testing which will be continued in next blog
